coredata.dc:title: Construction of Z-scheme heterojunction C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 for full-spectrum photocatalytic organic pollutant degradation
coredata.dc:creator: He, Yi
Huang, Ju
Wang, Binsong
Qu, Yang
coredata.dc:description: To achieve efficient solar-energy conversion, the development of full-spectrum light-driven photocatalysts with valid charge separation performance from UV to NIR wavelengths is a key challenge. Herein, a full-spectrum light-driven g-C3N4/nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots/W18O49 (C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49) ternary heterojunction catalyst was fabricated via a solvothermal and hydrothermal method. The prepared ternary heterojunction was shown to exhibit high photocatalytic activity under full-spectrum light, particularly in the NIR region, for degrading the organic pollutants methyl blue (MB), methyl orange (MO), ciprofloxacin (CIP) and tetracycline (TC). XRD, XPS and TEM analysis demonstrated that the synergistic effects of Z-scheme dual-channel charge transfer pathways and localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effects contributed to the high photocatalytic activity. Under UV–vis light irradiation, the Z-scheme pathway enhanced photogenerated charge separation, maintaining the redox of photogenerated electrons and holes for the catalytic reaction. The LSPR effect of the nonmetallic plasma W18O49 broadened the catalytic response to the NIR region, thus improving light utilization. The introduced N-CQDs provided an excellent photoelectron transport medium for electron-hole transfer and separation. This study established an efficient strategy for the preparation of full-spectrum-driven photocatalysts for organic pollutant degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Plasma W18O49
Full-spectrum light-driven photocatalyst
Antibiotic degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural analysis of the prepared photocatalysts

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Outstanding Youth Foundation of Heilongjiang University

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the NSFC project (21971057), with funding provided by the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (YQ2019B006), the Postdoctoral Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LBH-Q19052), the Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (2020-KYYWF-1008) and the Outstanding Youth Foundation of Heilongjiang University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of W18O49, C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 and C3N4 samples; (b) EDS analysis of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49.
(a, d) TEM image of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3; (b) HRTEM image of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3; (c) TEM image of N-CQDs; EDS mapping of (e) C; (f) W; (g) O; and (h) N.
FT-IR spectra of the W18O49, C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 and C3N4 samples.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) C 1s; (b) N 1s; (c) O 1s; and (d) W 4f for C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3.
Electrochemical characteristics of W18O49, C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 and C3N4 samples: (a) PL spectra; (b) photocurrent responses; (c) EIS Nyquist plots; (d) SPS responses.

Photocatalytic degradation by different photocatalysts under full-spectrum light irradiation (λ > 365 nm) conditions of (a) CIP and (b) TC. Kinetic constants of degradation by different photocatalysts under full-spectrum light irradiation (λ > 365 nm) conditions for (c) CIP and (d) TC.

Recycling performance of the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 nanocomposite for the degradation of CIP under (a) full spectrum irradiation; and (b) NIR irradiation conditions. Trapping experiments for the photocatalytic degradation of CIP under (c) full spectrum irradiation; and (d) NIR irradiation conditions.
The band gap energy of (a) C3N4 and (b) W18O49; VB XPS valence spectra of (c) C3N4 and (d) W18O49.
Schematic illustration of the photoexcited electron and hole separation process under (a) UV–vis light and (b) NIR light irradiation conditions.

head.ce:title: Construction of Z-scheme heterojunction C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 for full-spectrum photocatalytic organic pollutant degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Science, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry, Ministry of Education of the People’s Republic of China, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science
Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry
Ministry of Education of the People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Science, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry, Ministry of Education of the People’s Republic of China, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To achieve efficient solar-energy conversion, the development of full-spectrum light-driven photocatalysts with valid charge separation performance from UV to NIR wavelengths is a key challenge. Herein, a full-spectrum light-driven g-C3N4/nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots/W18O49 (C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49) ternary heterojunction catalyst was fabricated via a solvothermal and hydrothermal method. The prepared ternary heterojunction was shown to exhibit high photocatalytic activity under full-spectrum light, particularly in the NIR region, for degrading the organic pollutants methyl blue (MB), methyl orange (MO), ciprofloxacin (CIP) and tetracycline (TC). XRD, XPS and TEM analysis demonstrated that the synergistic effects of Z-scheme dual-channel charge transfer pathways and localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effects contributed to the high photocatalytic activity. Under UV–vis light irradiation, the Z-scheme pathway enhanced photogenerated charge separation, maintaining the redox of photogenerated electrons and holes for the catalytic reaction. The LSPR effect of the nonmetallic plasma W18O49 broadened the catalytic response to the NIR region, thus improving light utilization. The introduced N-CQDs provided an excellent photoelectron transport medium for electron-hole transfer and separation. This study established an efficient strategy for the preparation of full-spectrum-driven photocatalysts for organic pollutant degradation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalysis is a promising and sustainable technology that has the potential for widespread application to reduce environmental pollution and help overcome the global energy demand crisis [1,2]. However, despite the significant progress in this research field, practical applications have shown that semiconductor photocatalysts exhibit low photocatalytic efficiency. Poor light utilization and low charge separation and transfer efficiency are key obstacles to the construction of effective semiconductor photocatalysts [3,4]. Therefore, it is crucial to resolve these problems and develop broad-spectrum photocatalysts with high charge separation efficiency. The construction of heterostructures using energy band-matched semiconductors is considered to be an effective method to prevent charge carrier complexation, through the spatial separation of photogenerated electrons and holes [5–8]. However, the use of conventional heterojunctions and the limited photoreaction range of photocatalysts hinder the redox ability of photogenerated charges [9]. In contrast, newly developed Z-scheme heterojunctions that improve photocatalytic efficiency by promoting charge transfer in nanocomposites can reduce these problems and retain the powerful redox ability of photogenerated charges [10,11].

Graphitic carbon nitride (C3N4) has attracted much attention in recent years, science its photocatalytic potential and capacity to absorb visible light for hydrogen production were discovered by Wang et al. in 2009 [12]. The band gap structure of C3N4 can absorb visible light. Meanwhile its tri-s-triazine ring provides stability over a wide temperature range and in the presence of various chemical reagents [13,14]. In addition, C3N4 has a light-weight and highly porous structure [15–17], which is advantageous for practical applications [18], although its catalytic efficiency is limited by the rapid complexation of holes and electrons [19], low photoreaction range [20], scarcity of surface redox active sites and avoidable disordered structural defects [21]. W18O49 is a well-established WO3-x photocatalyst that is capable of light collection within a wide range from UV to NIR wavelengths, due to its high abundance of surface oxygen vacancies [22,23]. For example, Feng et al. reported that protection of the oxygen vacancy of W18O49 enhances its photocatalytic efficiency [24]. It has also been demonstrated by Deng et al. that C3N4@W18O49 itself is a direct Z-scheme photocatalyst, exhibiting high photodegradation efficiency due to the LSPR-inspired(LSPR originates from the local electronic confinement around the lattice W5+-W5+ pair in the unique structure of W18O49, independent of particle shape and size) ‘hot electron’ injection process, with the dual-charge carrier transfer strategy of the Z-scheme heterojunction promoting photogenerated charge separation and broadening the response spectrum to the full wavelength range [25]. However, despite extensive research having been performed in this field, there remain some key challenges to the application of direct Z-scheme C3N4@W18O49 photocatalytic heterojunctions. For example, photocatalytic efficiency is reduced by some of the defects formed between C3N4 and W18O49. Therefore, W18O49 became the recombination centers of electron-hole pairs, insufficient molecular oxygen activation, lattice mismatch and the lack of interfacial connections.

The charge-transfer kinetics of Z-scheme heterojunctions can be accelerated by introducing charge-transfer intermediates at the heterojunction interface [22]. Carbon dots (CDs) are low environmental impact nanocarbon materials. Due to their unique optical, and electronic properties, CDs have attracted considerable research attention, exhibiting excellent electron transfer and storage properties [26] with satisfactory performance as catalyst components or co-catalysts. Furthermore, CDs exhibit up-conversion photoluminescence properties [27,28]. When CDs are coupled with semiconductors such as C3N4 [29], TiO2 [30] and ZnO [31], the resulting nanocomposites can absorb longer visible light wavelengths and emit shorter UV light wavelengths, increasing the photogeneration of charge carriers and thus, improving the photocatalytic efficiency of these materials. In addition, the optical and electrical properties of CDs can be optimized by doping with N atoms due to their relatively strong electron affinity. The electrons on the CB of the semiconductor can easily be transferred to N-doped carbon dots (N-CQDs), leading to the efficient separation of electron-hole pairs. Therefore, as solid conductors, N-CQDs have the advantages of being low cost, with low toxicity, good atmospheric stability, stable photoluminescence with excellent electron transfer and energy storage properties [32,33]. The use of N-CQDs as a medium can reduce the electron transfer distance and consolidate Z-scheme charge transfer, effectively improving the charge transfer rate.

Herein, C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 nanocomposites with good full-spectrum photogenerated radiative properties were synthesized. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the prepared C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 nanocomposites was determined for the organic pollutants MB, MO, CIP and TC. In addition, visible and NIR experiments were performed on radical trapping to elucidate the mechanism of photocatalytic performance enhancement, with the unique charge transfer processes of this nanocomposite providing novel perspectives for photocatalyst design.
The aqueous solution used in all tests was deionized water and a detailed description of all other materials, reagents and equipment used is provided in the supplementary materials.

A schematic of the method used to synthesize the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 nanocomposites is shown in Fig. S1. First, C3N4 nanosheets and N-CQDs were synthesized and coupled, W18O49 was grown in situ and the final product was calcined to obtain the final samples. The synthesis method is described in detail in the Supplementary Materials.

The photocatalytic performance of the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 nanocomposites was determined based on the degradation of aqueous solutions containing a mixture of MB, MO, CIP and TC (pH = 6.5).The experimental setup was designed according to a previously reported method [34]. A detailed description of the experimental procedure is provided in the Supplementary Materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural analysis of the prepared photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the crystal structures of C3N4, W18O49 and C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3, the materials were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 1 a, the characteristic peak of C3N4 at 13.2° corresponds to the (100) crystal plane, and the characteristic peak exhibited at 27.6° corresponds to the (002) crystal plane. The characteristic peak at 13.2°was related to the plane of the tri-s-triazine unit, while the characteristic peak at 27.6°was related to the interface of the aromatic system [34,35]. The characteristic peak of W18O49 was clearly observed, as shown in Fig. 1a, which was presumed to belong to the monoclinic structure of W18O49 (JCPDS. No. 05-0392) [36]. The characteristic peaks of C3N4 and W18O49 were observed in the XRD plots of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49, indicating that C3N4 and W18O49 were successfully combined. However, the characteristic peaks of N-CQDs were not observed in the XRD plots of the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 photocatalyst, probably due to their low content, although their existence was confirmed later by TEM and XPS analysis. The EDS diagram of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 is shown in Fig. 1b, which shows that there are elements of C, N, O, W. It can be presumed to be a complex based on the synthesis experiment, and the formation of the complex was proven by XRD, XPS, TEM, etc.

The optical absorption capability of C3N4, W18O49 and C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 was determined by UV–vis-IR spectroscopy of absorptances. As shown in Fig. S2, C3N4 exhibited a strong absorption capability for light wavelengths in the visible range, with an absorption edge wavelength of approximately 460 nm due to the unique narrow band gap of C3N4 [37]. W18O49 exhibited a tail of unique absorption between 450 nm and 1500 nm, indicating that W18O49 had a strong absorption capability in the NIR region due to its LSPR properties caused by the collective oscillation of surface oxygen vacancies under the influence of visible and NIR irradiation [24]. In general, the absorption spectrum of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 was similar to that of C3N4, which was due to the dominance of the C3N4 component. In addition, the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 catalyst exhibited the capacity to absorb wavelengths from the visible to NIR ranges, suggesting that C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 effectively responded to the broad solar spectrum. The observed increase in the absorption capability of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 after heterojunction formation was attributed to the absorption of band-gap electrons in the UV–visible domain, meanwhile the enhanced absorption of NIR light was due to the metal-like LSPR effect caused by the surface oxygen vacancies of W18O49. As shown in Fig. S2, when the W18O49 content in C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 increased, the visible-NIR light response was weakened and eventually suppressed, affecting the photocatalytic ability of this nanocomposite.

To investigate the morphology of the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 photocatalyst, the nanocomposite was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The results of SEM imaging are shown in Fig. S3. C3N4 had a typical nanosheet structure, W18O49 has a rod-like structure and C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 as well, while N-CQDs were too small to be observed by SEM. Fig. 2 (a and d) presents the results of TEM imaging of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3, verifying the rod-like structure of W18O49. Fig. 2b shows a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3, exhibiting a high lattice structure with a W18O49 lattice spacing measured to be 0.38 nm [38], which was consistent with the (010) plane of W18O49. It was observed that the N-CQDs were attached to the surface of C3N4 nanosheets, as well as between W18O49 and C3N4 nanosheets. The immobilized N-CQDs served as mediators to promote the effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs during photocatalytic degradation, providing an interface on the nanostructures of C3N4 and W18O49 to form a solid ternary nanocomposite material. As shown in Fig. 2c, the diameter of the N-CQDs was approximately 2 nm, with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.32 nm [39], a uniform distribution, a good particle ratio, no overall aggregation and random distribution. In addition, HAADF-STEM imaging of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 (Fig. 2(e-h)) showed that the formed heterojunctions contained C, W, O, and N elements, with these elements being well distributed throughout the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 framework. These results provide evidence for the successful synthesis of the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 photocatalyst.

In the prepared photocatalytic materials, the characteristic signals of C3N4, W18O49 and C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 3 a. C3N4 nanosheets exhibited peaks at 809 cm−1 and 3172 cm−1, which were assigned to the characteristic breathing mode of the triazine unit and the stretching mode of NH in C3N4 [21]. The peaks present from 1240 cm−1 to 1640 cm−1 were associated with the typical stretching mode of the C3N4 heterocycle. The 591–812 cm−1 peak of W18O49 was attributed to the characteristic vibrations of W=O and WO [40], while the peak at 1616 cm−1 corresponded to –OH. The C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 photocatalyst exhibited characteristic FTIR peaks for C3N4 and W18O49, indicating the successful combination of these two components. However, no N-CQDs characteristic peak was observed for C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3, which may be of low N-CQDs content in the nanocomposites [40]. The feature FTIR curves of C3N4 and W18O49 in the prepared C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 materials were analyzed and compared in depth (Fig. 4 b), showing that the site of the s-triazine ring vibrational peak underwent a slight change, suggesting that the “nitrogen can” may have a structural deformation by chemical interaction between C3N4 and W18O49.

The surface chemical composition and state of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), The survey XPS in Fig. S4 means that the prepared C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 was mainly composed of C, N, O and W elements. with the high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and W 4f spectra shown in Fig. 4. There was a characteristic peak of amorphous carbon at 284.8 eV, which was used in the calibration of peaks [41] and although not shown in Fig. 4a, the characteristic peak of W18O49 was also calibrated according to the amorphous carbon peak. The C 1s spectrum of the C3N4 nanosheet had two peaks at 288.1 eV and 284.8 eV, with the 288.1 eV peak assigned to N-CN and the 284.8 eV peak assigned to sp2 heterogeneous CC bonding. As shown in Fig. 4b, the N 1s spectrum of C3N4 nanosheets exhibited three peaks at 398.6, 400.4 and 401.2 eV. The peak at 398.6 eV was attributed to sp2-hybridized nitrogen (C-NC) on the C-containing triazine ring, while the peak at 400.4 eV corresponded to tertiary nitrogen (N-(C)3) and the peak at 401.2 eV to the amino group (C-4558NH). As shown in Fig. 4c, the O 1s spectrum of the W18O49 sample contained peaks at 530.3 and 531.8 eV, with the peak at 530.3 eV assigned to W-O and the peak at 531.8 eV was assigned to –OH. The O 1s spectrum of the prepared C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 sample showed a new characteristic peak at 532.8 eV, which resulted from the interaction of various components of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3. Fig. 4d shows the XPS spectrum of W 4f, the peak at 35.69 eV corresponding to W6+ 4f5/2, the peak at 37.69 eV corresponding to W6+ 4f7/2, the peak at 34.34 eV corresponding to W5+ 4f5/2, and the peak at 36.64 eV corresponding to W5+ 4f7/2, respectively. W5+ is a metal-like LSPR effect was caused by the presence of surface oxygen vacancies on W18O49 [42]. The increased W5+ defect in C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 compared to W18O49 was mainly due to the charge transfer generated by the built-in electric field formed by the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 heterojunction structure, promoting the generation of hot electrons from surface oxygen vacancies on W18O49. The XPS images of the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 materials contained characteristic peaks for C3N4 and W18O49, further verifying the successful synthesis of the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 nanocomposite material. It can be seen from the Figure that after the successful synthesis of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3, the characteristic peaks of C3N4 and W18O49 shifted, with the binding energies of O 1s and W 4f increasing, and that of N 1s decreasing. It is well known that the higher the binding energy is, the lower the electron density and vice versa [25]; therefore, the energy of binding changes of N 1s, O 1s and W 4f in the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 material indicates that the electron density of C3N4 and W18O49 may have changed. Based on this potential change in binding energy, it can be inferred that in the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 nanocomposite, electron transfer occurs on the heterojunction and charges tends to shift from W18O49 to C3N4, providing evidence of the Z-scheme reaction mechanism.

The performance of photocatalysts is closely related to the migration and separation of light-generated electrons. Fig. 5 a shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of C3N4, W18O49 and C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3, with the fluorescence emission spectra measured at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. The PL intensity of C3N4 was the highest among all the prepared photocatalysts, while the PL intensity of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 was relatively low, indicating that the photogenerated charge rate of the nanocomposite materials was suppressed by the assembly of W18O49 onto the surface of C3N4 nanosheets. Furthermore, the loading of N-CQDs onto the C3N4 and W18O49 nanostructures also reduced the nanocomposites rate of photogeneration of electrons (e-) and holes (h+). W18O49 exhibited the lowest PL peak compared to C3N4 and C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3, although this did not automatically indicate that W18O49 exhibited the best charge separation rate, as different semiconductors have different PL responses. The results showed that the formation of the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 heterojunction reduced the complexation rate of photogenerated charges and holes, effectively improving charge transfer. Fig. 5b shows the instantaneous photocurrent response spectra for C3N4, W18O49 and C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3, revealing the photogenerated electron transfer characteristics of semiconductor materials [42]. The photocurrent intensity of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 was the highest among all the materials, followed by C3N4 and finally W18O49, which had the lowest intensity, indicating that C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 exhibits good photogenerated electron and hole separation rates. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is an electrochemical method used to measure the electron transfer rate of an electrode based on the radius of the arc. Fig. 5c shows the plots of EIS Nyquist plots obtained for the three semiconductor samples, among which the curve of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 was the smallest, indicating that the interfacial resistance of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 in the electrolyte solution was the lowest, which was consistent with the results of CIP and TC degradation. These findings showed that the formation of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 heterojunctions can improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges and holes, reducing their compounding efficiency and subsequently lowering the impedance of the semiconductor. Fig. 5d shows the surface photovoltaic spectrum (SPS) of C3N4, W18O49 and C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 under atmospheric conditions. C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 exhibited stronger SPS signatures, while the SPS signals of C3N4 and W18O49 were weak, suggesting that W18O49 and N-CQDs improved the charge separation of C3N4.

To further understand the catalytic performance of the prepared C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 catalysts, MB, MO, CIP and TC were decomposed. Among these antibiotics, CIP and TC are universal antibiotics. Adsorption equilibrium was first achieved in a dark environment. Fig. 6 illustrates the photocatalytic degradation results of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49, C3N4 and W18O49 on TC and CIP. As shown in Fig. 6a, the photocatalytic degradation of CIP under full spectrum (λ > 365 nm) irradiation conditions, reached degradation rates of 57.2 % and 54.9 % using C3N4 and W18O49 photocatalysts, respectively, while C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 achieved a degradation rate of 99.5 %. Fig. 6b presents the photocatalytic degradation of TC under full spectrum (λ > 365 nm) irradiation conditions, showing that C3N4 and W18O49 reached low TC degradation rates of 49.6 % and 45.4 %, respectively, while C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 exhibited high catalytic activity with 99.6 % TC degradation. Overall, the catalytic activities of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-1, C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 and C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-5 were much higher than those of C3N4 and W18O49, due to the quasi-metallic LSPR effect generated by surface oxygen vacancies on W18O49, which improved the charge separation efficiency of the photocatalyst. The catalytic activity of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 was found to decrease when the content of W18O49 in C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 was higher or lower than that of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3. This phenomenon was likely to occur because (1) when the content of W18O49 was lower than that of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3, C3N4 and W18O49 did not achieve an optimal synergistic effect, hindering the photogenerated electron and hole transfer efficiency; (2) when the content of W18O49 was higher than C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3, the active sites of C3N4 were covered by W18O49, which reduced the photodegradation efficiency; (3) The varying content of W18O49 changed the light absorption range of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49. The photocatalytic activities of C3N4, W18O49 and C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 were investigated further in NIR photodegradation experiments. C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 exhibited the highest catalytic activity for antibiotics degradation under NIR (λ > 710 nm) irradiation conditions (Fig. S5a), achieving 84.5 % CIP degradation and 83.4 % TC degradation (Fig. S5b). Almost no degradation without photocatalyst. This may be attributed to the LSPR effect, and the synergistic effect of the heterojunction and N-CQDs. The degradation rate constants under full spectrum (λ > 365 nm) irradiation conditions are shown in Fig. 6 (c and d), among which the rate constants of the nanocomposites were higher than C3N4 and W18O49. These results showed that C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 achieved good catalytic performance, indicating that these nanocomposites may be valuable for antibiotic pollutant degradation, although further research is required to optimize their use in practical applications.

Fig. S6 illustrated the degradation results of C3N4, W18O49 and C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 on MB and MO. As shown in Fig. S6a, the MB degradation rates achieved using C3N4 and W18O49 were 81.3 % and 62.3 %, respectively. Meanwhile,MB was almost completely degraded by C3N4/N-CQDs@ W18O49-3 under full spectral (λ > 365 nm) illumination conditions after 90 mins. The degradation of MO by C3N4, W18O49 and C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 were also investigated, with C3N4 and W18O49 degrading 53.8 % and 47.2 % of MO, respectively. Meanwhile, MO was almost completely degraded by C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 after 120 mins (Fig. S6b). The photocatalytic activities of C3N4, W18O49 and C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 were further investigated under NIR irradiation conditions. As shown in Fig. S6, C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 achieved a degradation rate of 83.7 % for MB and 82.8 % for MO, exhibiting a higher catalytic performance than C3N4 or W18O49. These results indicated that C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 catalysts could effectively degrade CIP, TC, MO and MB, illustrating the potential for application of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 in environmental pollution remediation.

For the realistic use of photocatalysts, the photocatalyst's stability and repeatability are important factors. Therefore, the stability of the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 material was evaluated with multiple reuse cycles for CIP degradation under full-spectrum light and NIR light irradiation conditions. As shown in Fig. 7 (a-b), the degradation capability of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 only slightly decreased after five cycles of reuse, indicating that the Z-scheme heterostructure of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 retained excellent photodegradation stability in the CIP solution under full-spectrum and NIR irradiation conditions. The photocatalysts were also subjected to XRD analysis before and after five cycles of reuse (Fig. S7), showing that both the original and reused samples exhibited similar characteristic peaks, with no other material peaks observed in the XRD patterns. These results further demonstrate that C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 has good stability and reusability properties in photocatalytic experiments.

To investigate the effect of other substances that were likely to coexist in the natural environment on the catalytic degradation of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49, the influence of pH and common anions was determined. Fig. S8 shows the degradation of antibiotics by C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 under different pH conditions, showing that under the conditions of full spectrum (λ > 365 nm) illumination the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 catalyst maintained good degradation ability for antibiotics across a wide pH range. With varying pH values, different TC species were formed, including negatively charged TC2- (pH > 9.68) and HTC- (7.68 < pH < 9.68), neutral H2TC0 (3.3 < pH < 7.68) and positively charged H3TC+ (pH < 3.3) [42]. Under alkaline conditions, the carboxyl group on the surface of C3N4 had an enhanced ability to deprotonate, interacting rapidly and electrostatically with the amino group of TC, while the generated ·OH attacked the piperazine ring, leading to piperazine ring oxidation and aromatic ring hydroxylation, decarboxylation, dealkylation and defluorination phenomena [43]. Therefore, C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 were shown to significantly improve the rate of TC degradation.

Cations and anions are usually present in water and coexist with the target pollutant. In order to explore the practical effects of photocatalysts, the effects of anions and cations on the decomposition of contaminants were analysed [44]. As indicated in Fig. S9, the presence of 2.5 mM K+, Na+, Ca2+ and Mg2+ had practically no hindrance on the degradation efficiency of TC or CIP. However, the degradation of CIP and TC was hindered when the photocatalytic reaction was carried out at 5 or 10 mM Cl- [45]. The source of the generated hindrance may be due to the oxidation of Cl- by ·OH produced in the decomposition, resulting in a large amount of ·Cl, which exhibits inferior performance to ·OH [46]. As indicated in Fig. S10, the degradation efficiency of CIP and TC was hardly hindered by SO4 2-. However, the degradation was hindered by the addition of HCO3 –, and the degradation rate of CIP and TC decreases with the increase of HCO3 – concentration, which may because HCO3 – was oxidized by ·OH to produce ·HCO3/·CO3 –. As ·HCO/·CO3 – reacts mainly with compounds of high electron density, its reactivity was lower than that of ·OH. As indicated in Fig. S11, the procedure process of CIP and TC was hardly hindered by different concentrations of NO3 –.

Comprehensive experimental results, the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 photocatalyst can effectively degrade CIP, TC, MO and MB under full-spectrum and NIR irradiation conditions. In addition, excellent photodegradation stability was demonstrated after four cycles of reuse. The C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 photocatalyst also exhibited good photocatalytic activity under full-spectrum irradiation and different pH conditions, as well as in the presence of inorganic salts, representing natural environmental conditions, demonstrating that C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 has good application prospects for use in environmental pollutant remediation.

The mechanism related to photocatalysis was analyzed by conducting degradation CIP radical trapping experiments on C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3. EDTA-2Na was used as a h+ trapping agent, BQ as an·O2 – trapping agent and IPA as an ·OH trapping agent [47]. The results of catalytic performance evaluation after radical capture are shown in Fig. 7. Fig. 7c shows that the degradation efficiency of CIP was significantly reduced after the incorporation of 1,4-benzoquinone, showing that·O2 – played an indispensable part in the decomposition of CIP under full-spectrum irradiation conditions. When EDTA-2Na was added, the photo-catalytic decomposition efficiency exhibited a lower level of reduction, showing that the effect of h+ on the degradation of CIP is smaller than that of ·O2 –., the degradation efficiency of CIP decreased from 98 % to 46 % after the addition of IPA, showing that the ·OH radical also had a significant impact on CIP degradation. Therefore, the ranked order of contribution of the three radicals upon UV–visible light illumination was ·O2 – >·OH > h+. To further investigate the reaction mechanism of the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 nanocomposite exposed to NIR radiation, radical trapping experiments were performed at λ > 710 nm. As shown in Fig. 7d, the presence of BQ and IPA significantly inhibited the photocatalytic degradation of CIP by C3N4/N-CQDs@ W18O49-3, indicating that ·O2 – and ·OH play an indispensable part in the NIR photodegradation process.

To determine the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) positions of C3N4 and W18O49, calculations were performed based on the Eg values and XPS data, as shown in Fig. 8 . The results presented in Fig. 8(a-b) show that the band gap energy values of C3N4 and W18O49 were 2.80 and 2.84 eV, respectively. The VB of the prepared C3N4 and W18O49 photocatalysts were also analyzed and the VB values of C3N4 and W18O49 were calculated from the XPS data, as shown in Fig. 8(c-d). According to the equation CB = VB-Eg, the VB value of C3N4 was 1.78 eV, while for W18O49 it was 3.56 eV and the CB value of C3N4 was calculated to be −1.02 eV, while for W18O49 it was 0.72 eV. To further illustrate the degradation mechanism of the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 catalyst, their redox potentials for the generation of photoelectric charges and holes as well as the electron transport status were analyzed. For W18O49, its VB photoexcited holes can oxidize the adsorbed H2O to produce ·OH (standard redox potential of 2.4 eV vs NHE for OH–/H2O). Since the CB of W18O49 is a correction potential, the photo-generated charge in the CB of W18O49 cannot reduce O2 to ·O2 – (standard oxidation potential of −0.33 eV vs NHE for O2/·O2 –), while the CB of C3N4 allows its photo-excited charge to reduce O2 to produce ·O2 –,but the VB of C3N4 whose photo-excited hole cannot produce ·OH.

Based on the above experimental results, the mechanism of degradation by the C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 nanostructure was proposed, as shown in Fig. 9 . Under full-spectrum illumination of light conditions, both C3N4 and W18O49 could be excited to generate photo-generated charges and holes. As ·O2– and ·OH radical production was demonstrated by free radical entrapment experiments, the conventional II heterojunction system cannot explain the mechanism of response. If the prepared C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 nanocomposite exhibited the conventional heterojunction system, the photogenerated electrons would tend to transfer from the CB of C3N4 to the CB of W18O49, while the photogenerated holes would tend to migrate from the W18O49 VB to the C3N4 VB. However, ·OH cannot be formed in C3N4 VB and ·O2 – cannot be formed in W18O49 CB. Combined with the results of XPS analysis, the charge transport pathway from W18O49 to C3N4 (channel I) was assessed (Fig. 9a), indicating that the Z-scheme may explain the high photocatalytic activity of C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3. Meanwhile, the excess electrons on C3N4 or W18O49 can be conducted to N-CQDs to further promote the transfer of photogenerated carriers. Therefore, under UV and visible illumination, both C3N4 and W18O49 are activated, and electrons on CB in W18O49 are transported to VB on C3N4 to bind to holes, with N-CQDs between the C3N4 and W18O49 layers acting as an electron transport medium, which accelerates charge migration. The electrons generated by photoexcitation remain in the CB of C3N4, showing a promising reduction effect and reacting with O2 to produce ·O2 –, while the holes generated by photoexcitation remain in the VB of W18O49, showing a promising oxidation ability and producing ·OH. In addition, the surface oxygen vacancies on W18O49 produce an LSPR effect for long-wave illumination conditions, hot generation of electrons (channel II) (Fig. 9b) which are injected into the CB of C3N4, increasing the electron density and promoting the photocatalytic performance. Meanwhile, the excess electrons on W18O49 can be conducted on the N-CQDs to further promote the transfer of photogenerated carriers.

The reaction mechanism of the prepared C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 nanocomposite was found to differ when subjected to NIR illumination (λ ≥ 700 nm). Under NIR illumination conditions, only W18O49 could be excited to generate photo-generated charges and holes, while radical trapping experiments confirmed the generation of ·OH and ·O2 – radicals. Under NIR irradiation conditions, W18O49 could be excited and the holes generated by the light could react with OH– or H2O to form ·OH. Due to the presence of oxygen vacancies, the LSPR effect generates hot electrons and as the potential of W18O49 is more negative than the O2 /·O2 – potential (-0.33 eV), Hot electrons oscillation to CB of C3N4 and solubilized oxygen reaction to produce ·O2 –.This phenomenon leads to the prepared C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 nanocomposite photocatalyst having enhanced photocatalytic activity under NIR irradiation conditions.

In summary, the dual-channel charge carrier transport pathways are based on the carrier transport of Z-scheme heterostructure semiconductor path junctions, with non-metallic LSPR-induced ‘hot electron’ injection leading to C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 nanocomposites having high photocatalytic activities. Furthermore, the nanocomposites were optimized to further improve the utilization of light by C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3, resulting in a large increase in the photogenerated electron and hole transfer rates and realizing the formation of an efficient full-spectrum driven photocatalyst. Thus, the dual-channel pathway of charge transfer helps to improve the photo-catalytic efficiency of the prepared C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49-3 nanocomposites.
(1) W18O49 + hυ = [e- CB ]W18O49 + [h+ VB]W18O49
(2) C3N4 + hυ = [e- CB ]C3N4 + [h+ VB]C3N4
(3) [e- CB ]W18O49 → [e- VB]C3N4
(4) [e- VB]C3N4 + [h+ VB]C3N4 → hυ
[e- CB]C3N4 + [O2](ad.)→·O2 –(5)
(6) [h+ VB]W18O49 + H2O→·OH
(7) Pollutants+·OH → Degraded products
(8) Pollutants+·O2 –→Degraded products
Where, [e- CB], [e- VB] and [h+ VB] indicate e− and h+ on the VB and CB, respectively.

In conclusion, novel Z-scheme C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 nanocomposites were successfully synthesized. The prepared C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 nanocomposites exhibited superior photocatalytic efficiency under both NIR and full-spectrum irradiation conditions, achieving high removal efficiencies for MB, MO, CIP and TC. The excellent catalytic effect between the prepared C3N4/N-CQDs@W18O49 nanocomposites was attributed to the unique dual-channel electronic carrier transport pathway, improving charge carrier separation via the Z-scheme and plasma-induced ‘hot electron’ injection process. Furthermore, the introduction of N-CQDs formed an interface that reduced the electron transport distance and improved electron-hole separation, consolidating the Z-scheme charge transfer and thus improving the utilization of optical energy. Our work provides a new insight into the combination of the outstanding electron properties of nonmetal plasmons and the highly efficient charge transfer Z-scheme reaction mechanism for the design and development of highly efficient photocatalysts, and promotes the development of the next generation of low-cost highly efficient Z-scheme photocatalysts for future contaminant removal and environmental remediation.

Yi He: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Ju Huang: Visualization, Investigation. Binsong Wang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yang Qu: Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: ZnO/carbon quantum dots nanocomposites: one-step fabrication and superior photocatalytic ability for toxic gas degradation under visible light at room temperature
